1: Chapter 29 returns to the Philistine military context (interleaved with 1 Samuel 28's temporal setting). ʾăpēq (Aphek) is the marshaling point in the coastal plain; ʿên ʾăšer beyizrəʿēl (the spring at Jezreel) is the Israelite position. The geography establishes the forces converging for Gilboa.
2: waʾădōnê pəlištîm ʿōḇərîm lamēʾôt wəlāʾălāpîm — "the Philistine lords passing in review by hundreds and thousands." The administrative review format mirrors Israelite mustering language (cf. 1 Samuel 11:8; 1 Samuel 15:4) — the Philistines have a structured military hierarchy. David and his men march at the rear with Achish — the most exposed position, furthest from the lords who will object.
3: ʾêleh hāʿiḇrîm māh lāhem — "What are these Hebrews doing here?" ʿIḇrî (H5680, Hebrew) — a term used by outsiders for Israelites; it carries a social-class or ethnic-distinction connotation rather than being the covenant name. Achish's defense of David — lōʾ māṣāʾtî bô məʾûmāh miyyôm nāpəlô ʿāday (I have found nothing against him from the day he "fell" to me) — uses nāpal ʾel (fell to, defected to), the standard idiom for changing sides.
4: wəhāyāh lāmô ləśāṭān bamməlḥāmāh — "he could turn against us in battle" (literally "he will become a śāṭān to us" — an adversary/accuser). The commanders use śāṭān (H7854, accuser/adversary) precisely: David as a potential fifth column. Their concern — ûbammāh yitrāṣeh ʾel ʾădōnāyw (how better to win back his lord's favor?) — correctly reads the political calculus: David would need to demonstrate loyalty to Saul by turning on the Philistines.
5: The commanders cite the battle-hymn of 1 Samuel 18:7: hikkāh šāʾûl bəʾălāpāyw wədāwid bərəḇāḇōtāyw — "Saul has killed his thousands, David his ten thousands." The song that made Saul jealous (1 Samuel 18:8-9) now surfaces as evidence that David is more dangerous than any ally. The song that nearly destroyed David in Saul's court now saves him from fighting against his own people.
6: ḥay YHWH kî yāšār ʾattāh — "As the LORD lives, you have been trustworthy/upright" (yāšār, H3477, straight/upright). Achish swears by YHWH — a pagan king invoking Israel's God — to attest David's innocence. ʾên māṣāʾtî bəḵā rāʿāh — "I have found nothing wrong with you" — the forensic formula of acquittal. The double attestation (Achish at vv3, 6, 9) places a threefold hostile-witness declaration of David's innocence in the record. The irony: David is publicly exonerated by a Philistine lord at the very moment Saul is seeking illegal counsel to face the same battle.
7: wəšûḇ ûlēḵ bəšālôm — "return and go in peace." The formula of peaceful dismissal releases David without dishonor. The narrative does not record David's inner response to being dismissed — whether relief or frustration is left to the reader, though the following chapter (Ziklag) shows the cost of the absence.
8: David's protest uses the formula of covenant service: meh māṣāʾtā bəʿabdəḵā — "what have you found against me?" The same language as Achish's own attestation (v6). Whether David genuinely wished to fight or was performing a necessary deception is left ambiguous; the text neither condemns nor commends the protestation.
9: ṭôḇ ʾattāh bəʿênay kəmalʾaḵ ʾĕlōhîm — "you are as good in my sight as a messenger from God (malʾaḵ ʾĕlōhîm)." Malʾaḵ ʾĕlōhîm (H4397 + H430) — divine messenger/angel — is the highest praise Achish can offer: David is like a being from the divine realm in his trustworthiness. The phrase uses the same ʾĕlōhîm that appeared in 28:13 for the apparition at Endor; the juxtaposition — a spirit rising from the earth there, a trustworthy servant compared to a heavenly messenger here — frames the theological contrast between the two kings.
10: wəhiškamtem babbōqer — "rise early in the morning." The command matches the narrative tempo: decisions in crisis must be executed quickly. David leaves without fighting either Israel or Philistia — his dilemma resolved by the very commanders who opposed him, with no moral compromise required of him.
11: The brief closing verse — David and his men return to Philistia, the Philistines march to Jezreel — leaves the two trajectories proceeding simultaneously. The reader knows both outcomes: David will face Ziklag (1 Samuel 30), and the Philistines will face Saul at Gilboa (1 Samuel 31). The narrative interleaving creates dramatic irony.
